coredata.dc:title: Microscale investigation of DNAPL displacement by engineered graphene quantum dots in heterogeneous porous media
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Rane, Kaustubh
Zhang, Bingjun
Goual, Lamia
coredata.dc:description: This x-ray microtomography study investigates the dynamic pore-scale displacement of a dense non-aqueous phase liquid (DNAPL) such as heavy crude oil in a heterogeneous aquifer rock using carbonaceous nanoparticles. The nanoparticles were synthesized from Wyoming coal and consisted of a mixture of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and engineered graphene quantum dots (E-GQD) in equal proportions. Synergistic interactions between GQD and E-GQD at the oil/water interface reduced the DNAPL/brine interfacial tension (IFT) from 13.4 mN/m to 5.4 mN/m. In addition, the nanofluid mixture altered the wettability of the minerals found in the rock (quartz, carbonate, and feldspar) from oil-wet to water-wet with average contact angles of 66°, 51°, and 60°, respectively. The wettability alteration was more pronounced in carbonates due to the tendency of GQD to adsorb on these surfaces. Analyses of the saturation profiles and pore-scale fluid occupancy maps revealed that the wettability alteration was not instantaneous and required a short soaking time for the fluid-rock interactions to take place. IFT reduction alone was not enough to displace the DNAPL at the early stages of nanofluid flooding because of the high density and viscosity of the oil. As a result, the nanofluid did not outperform brine after 1 pore volume (PV) of injection. On the other hand, the nanofluid was able to invade small pores that were not accessible to brine after 3 PV of injection due to both IFT and contact angle reduction, which lowered the threshold capillary pressure of these pores. Subsequently, more medium and large pores were invaded by the nanofluid as the injection continued, leading to an 11% increase in DNAPL recovery after 20 PV of nanofluid injection, as compared to waterflooding. This incremental recovery is significant considering that the experiments were performed with a heavy oil at ambient temperature, which is more representative of aquifer conditions. Thus, the GQD-based nanofluid has the potential to achieve much larger recoveries in deeper heavy oil reservoirs provided enough soaking time is allowed. The insights provided by this study could be used to validate pore-scale network models or guide in the design and selection of more effective nanomaterials for aquifer remediation or enhanced oil recovery applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Crude oil
Coal
Flow through porous media
X-ray microtomography
Wettability
Pore-scale displacement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dynamic interfacial tension and adsorption measurements

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Science Foundation Directorate for Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the U.S. National Science Foundation , USA (NSF Career Award # 1351296 ) for financial support and Dr. Mohammad Piri for providing access to the Center of Innovation for Flow through Porous Media (COIFPM). The authors also would like to thank Omar Elmasry for conducting the QEMSCAN analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mineralogy map of Arkose by QEMSCAN.
Segmentation of minerals (a,b,g), resulting pore space and quartz, carbonate, and feldspar minerals (c–f).
(a) Porosity distribution in different sized cubic samples, (b) pore size distribution of Arkose, and (c) cumulative volume with increase in pore size.
(a) Dynamic interfacial tension between DNAPL and brine/nanofluid, (b) adsorption of nanofluid on silica and calcite substrates.
Water saturation along the core for (a) waterflooding and (b) nanofluid flooding, and (c) cumulative oil recovery versus injected PV of brine and nanofluid.
Contact angle distribution on (a) quartz, (b) carbonate, (c) feldspar before and after waterflooding.
In situ 3 phase (NAPL-water-mineral) contact angles before and after waterflooding.
(a) In-situ contact angle distribution on quartz, carbonate, and feldspar at different stages of nanofluid flooding, and (b) contact angle variation between each stage.
In-situ 3 phase (NAPL-water-mineral) contact angles at different stages of nanofluid flooding.
(a) Cumulative DNAPL cluster size distribution after different flooding experiments, and (b) 3D visualization examples of oil clusters.
3D visualization of fluid occupancy after 3 PV of waterflooding (a) and nanofluid flooding (b), and after 20 PV of waterflooding (c) and nanofluid flooding (d).
Difference in cumulative oil recovery between nanofluid and brine in large, medium and small pores after different pore volumes of fluid injection. Inset shows examples of pore types.

head.ce:title: Microscale investigation of DNAPL displacement by engineered graphene quantum dots in heterogeneous porous media

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Petroleum Engineering, University of Wyoming, Laramie, WY 82071, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Petroleum Engineering, University of Wyoming

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Petroleum Engineering, University of Wyoming, Laramie, WY 82071, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This x-ray microtomography study investigates the dynamic pore-scale displacement of a dense non-aqueous phase liquid (DNAPL) such as heavy crude oil in a heterogeneous aquifer rock using carbonaceous nanoparticles. The nanoparticles were synthesized from Wyoming coal and consisted of a mixture of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and engineered graphene quantum dots (E-GQD) in equal proportions. Synergistic interactions between GQD and E-GQD at the oil/water interface reduced the DNAPL/brine interfacial tension (IFT) from 13.4 mN/m to 5.4 mN/m. In addition, the nanofluid mixture altered the wettability of the minerals found in the rock (quartz, carbonate, and feldspar) from oil-wet to water-wet with average contact angles of 66°, 51°, and 60°, respectively. The wettability alteration was more pronounced in carbonates due to the tendency of GQD to adsorb on these surfaces. Analyses of the saturation profiles and pore-scale fluid occupancy maps revealed that the wettability alteration was not instantaneous and required a short soaking time for the fluid-rock interactions to take place. IFT reduction alone was not enough to displace the DNAPL at the early stages of nanofluid flooding because of the high density and viscosity of the oil. As a result, the nanofluid did not outperform brine after 1 pore volume (PV) of injection. On the other hand, the nanofluid was able to invade small pores that were not accessible to brine after 3 PV of injection due to both IFT and contact angle reduction, which lowered the threshold capillary pressure of these pores. Subsequently, more medium and large pores were invaded by the nanofluid as the injection continued, leading to an 11% increase in DNAPL recovery after 20 PV of nanofluid injection, as compared to waterflooding. This incremental recovery is significant considering that the experiments were performed with a heavy oil at ambient temperature, which is more representative of aquifer conditions. Thus, the GQD-based nanofluid has the potential to achieve much larger recoveries in deeper heavy oil reservoirs provided enough soaking time is allowed. The insights provided by this study could be used to validate pore-scale network models or guide in the design and selection of more effective nanomaterials for aquifer remediation or enhanced oil recovery applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the physics and interactions involved during multi-phase fluid flow in porous media is particularly important for a number of applications such as enhanced oil recovery (EOR), aquifer remediation, CO2 sequestration, etc [1–3]. During these processes, an aqueous phase is injected into porous media to displace non-aqueous phase liquids (NAPL). The flow and interactions of immiscible fluids are at the heart of these applications. Wettability, defined as the relative affinity of the fluids to the solid surfaces, has a significant impact on the displacement of these immiscible fluids [4,5]. The degree of surface wettability is strongly affected by the adsorption/desorption of heavy oil constituents (e.g., resins and asphaltenes), rock mineralogy, and spreading capability of the oleic phase [6]. Depending on the wettability of the surface, the NAPL displacement can be characterized as a drainage or an imbibition process. Drainage is defined as a flow process where the non-wetting fluid displaces the wetting fluid, while imbibition is a process where the non-wetting fluid is being displaced by the wetting fluid. During the brine flooding of an oil-wet pore space, which is a typical drainage process, the non-wetting brine phase occupies the center of the pores to minimize its contact with the rock surface, leaving the NAPL in the corners and crevices untouched. In this process, the non-wetting phase also tends to invade into the larger pores due to their lower threshold capillary pressure, while the wetting phase remains in the smaller pores and crevices of the larger pores [7–10]. The selective advance through larger pores causes the formation of discontinuous flow patterns trapping various NAPL ganglia after waterflood [11]. Therefore, the reduction of trapped ganglia inside the rock is a key parameter to enhancing NAPL recovery, which was observed using fast synchrotron X-ray computed tomography (CT) [12].

While wettability involves fluid-solid interactions, interfacial tension (IFT), which is another significant parameter in capillarity, describes fluid-fluid interactions. In capillary-dominated flow regimes, fluid mobilization is directly related to the capillary number, which is the ratio of viscous forces to capillary forces [13]. Surfactants are widely used for NAPL mobilization because of their ability to reduce the IFT, leading to lower capillary forces or higher capillary numbers [14]. In addition, IFT reduction promotes NAPL emulsification in the aqueous phase, which has been reported as a possible mechanism for enhanced recovery [1,15–17]. A recent microtomography study has shown that when the surfactant concentration is above the critical micelle concentration (CMC), it instantly emulsifies the NAPL upon injection into the porous media [18]. The surfactant thus assists in the mobilization of the NAPL ganglia left behind after waterflooding [19].

In addition to surfactants, metal oxide nanoparticles (NPs) are often used to alter the wettability and solubilize NAPL from rock surfaces [20–22]. Their large surface area to volume ratio allows for maximum interactions with NAPL and minerals, endowing them with high interfacial activity. The mechanism of wettability alteration may be triggered by the adsorption of nanoparticles on the rock or the ability to structure themselves at the rock-fluid interface and form wedge-shaped films that increase the structural disjoining pressure [23]. However, a major challenge encountered with metal oxide (MO) nanoparticles is their tendency to agglomerate in high salinity brines due to electrical double layer compression, leading to pore plugging and formation damage [20]. To overcome this problem, carbonaceous nanoparticles are emerging as a sustainable alternative to their metal oxide counterparts. Graphene-based nanoparticles, in particular, possess oxygenated functional groups and abundant active edge sites that allow them to be thermally reduced or chemically functionalized [23]. The asymmetrical modification of the graphitic backbone can lead to amphiphilic or Janus structures with increased interfacial activity and colloidal stability [23,24]. Following this concept, graphene oxide (GO) was asymmetrically functionalized on starch templates to obtain Janus or engineered carbon nanosheets (E-CNS) with amphiphilic character [25–27]. The nanosheets were able to mobilize and solubilize more NAPL than base brine by further lowering the IFT and altering the wettability of the rock [21,24]. Recently, engineered graphene quantum dots (E-GQD) were synthesized following a similar approach and found to be more stable than E-CNS because of their smaller average size (3 nm vs. 100 nm) [28,29]. Nanofluids consisting of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and E-GQD blends in equal proportion significantly reduced the IFT (more than nine times that with E-CNS) due to synergistic effects at the interface [25,29]. Furthermore, E-GQD had almost no impact on wettability because of their hydrophobic aliphatic groups, which provided steric hindrance towards the mineral surfaces. GQD, on the other hand, adsorbed more on calcite than quartz, leading to potential pore plugging and lower NAPL recoveries in carbonates as compared to silicates (9.6 vs. 21%) [29].

The goal of this study was to extend our previous work to more heterogeneous rocks and denser fluids and to investigate the dynamics of nanofluid-enhanced fluid displacement under these conditions. This study is fundamentally novel in that it addresses the pore-scale displacement mechanisms of dense non-aqueous phase liquids (DNAPL) in heterogeneous porous media using new engineered graphene quantum dots. The pore-scale displacement mechanism was investigated by direct in-situ contact angle measurements, oil ganglia distribution frequency analysis, and 3D visualization of pore occupancy maps of oil and water using a state-of-the-art core flooding system integrated with a micro-CT scanner. We show for the first time that engineered graphene quantum dots can lower the interfacial tension and alter the wettability of all the minerals present in Arkose rock provided enough soaking time is allowed. The incremental recovery generated by these nanofluids through pores of various sizes is significant considering that the experiments were performed with a heavy oil at ambient conditions.

The DNAPL phase used in this study was a heavy crude oil obtained from the Milne Point field located northwest of Prudhoe Bay in Alaska. The oil had a density of 0.92 g/cm3 and a viscosity of 107.56 cP at ambient conditions (20 °C). It was doped with 14 vol% Iodooctane (98%, Sigma Aldrich) to improve the contrast between the oleic and aqueous phases in the micro-CT images. Carbon dioxide or CO2 (industrial grade, Airgas) was used in the flooding tests, whereas 1 wt% Alconox solution in water (pH 9.5, Alconox) and nitrogen (ultra-high purity, Airgas) were used in the adsorption tests. The brine consisted of 1 M NaCl in deionized (DI) water. This salinity was several orders of magnitude higher than those used in previous studies with E-CNS and MO nanoparticles [20,25]. The nanofluid aqueous phase was prepared by dispersing 0.1 wt% of nanoparticles in 1 M NaCl brine. The nanoparticles consisted of a mixture of GQD and E-GQD with a weight ratio of 1/1. Details on the synthesis and properties of the nanofluid can be found in our previous study [29]. In short, GQDs were synthesized by oxidative cleavage of coal by hydrogen peroxide. The E-GQDs were obtained by asymmetrically modifying the GQD through grafting a dodecyl amine chain on one side of the nanoparticle using a starch template [29].

Highly heterogeneous Arkose rock blocks were collected from Owl Canyon in northern Colorado near the boundaries of the Casper aquifer. A parent core of 1.5-inch length and 3-inch diameter was drilled from an Arkose block, and its petrophysical properties were measured using a helium porosimeter (Coretest Systems AP-608). The average porosity and permeability of the parent core were 20.2% and 73.45 mD, respectively. A smaller core specimen of 5 mm in diameter and 4.6 cm in length was drilled and cut to be used in the micro-CT experiment. The porosity of the miniature core was obtained by analyzing dry scan images, and the permeability was calculated from Darcy’s law by measuring pressure drops across the core at different flow rates. The porosity and permeability of this core were 12.9% and 24.9 mD, respectively, which were different from those of the parent core due to rock heterogeneity in terms of pore space distribution.

Quantitative Evaluation of Minerals by Scanning electron microscopy (QEMSCAN) was used to obtain a quantitative analysis of minerals in the rock sample. The instrument uses Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) and backscattered-electron image (BEI) from each measurement point to provide information about the underlying minerals [30]. In this study, QEMSCAN 650F by FEI was used with an X-ray source at 15 kV and a beam size value of 5.8. The 2D scanned area of the rock was 3 mm × 3 mm with a pixel resolution of 2 µm. Fig. 1 shows the mineral distribution results of the QEMSCAN for Arkose. The major minerals were quartz (45.06%), dolomite (12.54%), calcite (9.05%), mica (9.05%), and feldspar (6.52%). The minor minerals were apatite, rutile, clays, and kaolinite. Mica and feldspar were mostly intertwined between other minerals, and thus only a few moieties were independent in the CT scan. Pores occupied 15.66% of the area. Based on the QEMSCAN data, the micro-CT scanned images were divided into pores, quartz, carbonates (dolomite and calcite), and feldspar (mica and feldspar). The separation of minerals is based on the difference in X-ray absorption. The gray-scale intensities are dependent on the atomic numbers of the elements in the minerals [31], i.e., the higher the molecular weight of the minerals, the higher the gray-scale intensity. Thus, quartz had the lowest gray-scale intensity, followed by the carbonates and feldspar. Fig. 2 shows the 3D images of the segmentation of rock based on gray-scale intensities and the resulting pore space and minerals.

To ensure that the scanned area is larger than the porosity-based Representative Elementary Volume (REV), the porosity was measured for varying cubic volumes with sides from 0.16 mm to 2.02 mm. Fig. 3(a) shows the variation of porosity with respect to the cubic volume of the scan. It is obvious that the porosity remains constant from 2.02 mm to 0.844 mm and starts varying below this value. This suggests that the REV for the rock sample is 0.601 mm3. The scanned volume for this work is 37.36 mm3, while the analyzed volume is 12.11 mm3. Both the scanned and analyzed volumes are much higher than the REV. Therefore, the analyzed results in this study are representative of the entire rock.

The pore size distribution for Arkose rock was determined from the dry scan of the core sample. Pores were separated from the grains, and their diameters were measured. Despite the fact that the rock is heterogeneous with respect to minerals, the pore volume is predominantly dominated by pores in the range of 50–180 µm. Fig. 3(b) shows the variation of volume occupancy of pores versus pore diameter, while Fig. 3(c) shows the cumulative volume over pore size.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic interfacial tension and adsorption measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Kruss Spinning Drop Tensiometer was used to measure the dynamic interfacial tension (IFT) between the DNAPL and brine/nanofluid at ambient temperature and pressure conditions. The fluid was loaded into the capillary, and the oil drop was launched into the tube at 8500 rpm. The IFT was then measured at a rotational speed of 14,500 rpm, and its value was determined by analyzing the shape of the drop using the ADVANCE software.

Adsorption measurements were conducted using a Quartz Crystal Microbalance with Dissipation monitoring (QCM-D, Biolin Scientific) on silica and calcite substrates. The substrates were cleaned with 1% Alconox solution for 30 mins, rinsed with DI water, and dried with nitrogen. The substrates were then placed in a UV ozone cleaner for 15 mins before using them in the measurements. Water was first flown into the QCM-D chamber to obtain a stable baseline before injecting the nanofluid. The viscoelastic model was used to analyze the data due to the spreading of the frequency and dissipation harmonics.

Microscale core flooding experiments were conducted in a high-resolution Zeiss x-ray micro-CT scanner (VERSA-XRM50) integrated with a high-precision miniature core flooding system. The schematic of the setup is shown in Fig. S1(a) of the Supporting Information [32]. The micro-flooding system can consecutively scan the core with more than 180° rotation. Five dual cylinder quizix pumps were used in the core flooding system to deliver fluids, apply axial confining pressure, and maintain backpressure throughout the experiments. The fluids (brine, DNAPL, and nanofluid) were injected from the bottom of the core holder while the effluent was collected from the top using a backpressure pump set at 300 psig pressure. The core inside the core holder was confined firmly by a net axial confining pressure of 200 psig through the confining pump, thereby ensuring that there was no slip or motion, which may affect the image quality of the core specimen during the scanning process.

The Supporting Information contains a schematic of the experimental procedure in Fig. S1(b) and a detailed explanation of the consecutive steps used in the flooding experiments. The flow was maintained under a capillary-dominated regime with a fluid flow rate of 0.001 cm3/min. In both waterflooding and nanofluid flooding tests, the core was scanned at 1, 3, 10, and 20 PV injection, respectively. DNAPL saturation, as well as in situ contact angles, were analyzed at the end of each flooding step.

A cylindrical part in the center of the core (3.3 mm in diameter and 3.2 mm in length) was scanned under high-resolution Zeiss micro-CT with a voxel size of 1.667 µm. The scanned cylindrical part was located at 21.6 mm from the inlet of the core holder and 22.7 mm from the outlet side of the core holder. This location was carefully adjusted at the concentric center of the 5-mm-diameter core as well as at the middle part of the 47.4-mm-long core sample, thereby minimizing the capillary end effect. At the experimental steps (described above in the Experimental Procedure section), multiple scans were performed to get the pore-scale fluid occupancy maps for investigating the underlying subtle interactions that dominating the recovery enhancement mechanism of the E-GQD. The scanned images were reconstructed through the Zeiss Shout software before being transferred to Avizo 2020.2 software for further image analysis. The core sample shifts in different experimental steps were closely monitored and carefully controlled accordingly to ensure that the same locations were imaged during different steps. In the image interpretation procedure, 150 slices on each side of the core were cropped to ensure the high quality of the analyzed results. In the next step, the two-phase scan was registered and aligned on the wet scan. It is worth noting that different two-phase scans were registered on the same wet scan to make sure that the same pores were compared in different scans. Subsequently, the pore map was extracted from the wet scan by an interactive thresholding tool. The obtained pore map was then multiplied with the registered two-phase images to get the fluid inside the pore. The fluid was then separated by intensity (brine with low intensity while doped DNAPL with high intensity) with an interactive thresholding tool. Since the Arkose core consists of different mineral groups, different intensity values were displayed on the matrix map, thereby allowing us to separate different minerals through different corresponding intensity values. By comprehensively analyzing X-ray adsorption/emission/reflection efficiency, elemental composition, and QEMSCAN map of different minerals, three minerals, quartz, carbonate (calcite and dolomite), feldspar, were identified in the matrix map. Mineral maps, DNAPL maps, and brine maps were added together to obtain the final map for further analysis. More details on data acquisition and image analysis can be found elsewhere [21]. Using these maps, the contact angles were manually measured in-situ at the triple contact regions between the aqueous phase, DNAPL phase, and the rock at the arc menisci and main terminal menisci [33]. To ensure data reproducibility, at least 60 contact angles were measured at randomly selected locations on each mineral at the end of each flooding step.

Fig. 4(a) displays the dynamic IFT between the DNAPL and the aqueous phase. The nanofluid was able to reduce the IFT from about 14 mN/m to 5 mN/m. The GQD and E-GQD arranged themselves in an ordered alternating fashion to minimize the repulsive forces between their aliphatic chains and maximize IFT reduction [28]. The adsorption profiles of the nanofluid on quartz and calcite substrates are provided in Fig. 4(b). The nanofluid exhibited similar adsorption behavior on both substrates with fast initial adsorption followed by a stabilization. The nanofluid adsorbed twice as much on calcite than on silica. The negatively charged GQD adsorbed strongly on the positively charged calcite due to electrostatic forces. Conversely, the adsorption of GQD on quartz was due to weaker hydrogen bonding forces, which led to lower adsorption [29].

After vacuum saturating the core completely with brine, primary drainage was performed by injecting DNAPL into the core. The brine inside the pores was displaced by the oil to reach an initial water saturation (Swi) of 14.5%. This saturation was an average value of all the pores inside the core. The core was then dynamically aged at Swi to alter the wettability of the rock from water-wet to oil-wet conditions. Waterflooding with 1 M NaCl brine was carried out after four weeks of aging, and the saturation profiles are shown in Fig. 5(a). A flow rate of 0.001 cm3/min was used, which corresponded to a capillary number of 1.88 × 10−7. The flow was in the capillary-dominated regime since the capillary number was lower than 10−6 [34].

During the waterflooding, the core was sequentially scanned after the injection of 1 PV, 3 PV, 8 PV, and 20 PV of brine to monitor the pore-scale changes at different stages of the waterflood. Fig. 5(a) shows the water saturation along the core, and Fig. 5(c) depicts the cumulative recovery at different stages of the flood. The first PV of brine injection recovered 14.1% of the original oil in place (OOIP). In the next 2 PVs, the rate of recovery decreased to only 3.9% of OOIP. With continued 3–8 PV injection, an additional 6.9% of OOIP was obtained. Thereafter, there was almost no change at 20 PV, with a final recovery of 26.1%. In this flooding experiment, most DNAPL was recovered in the first PV after breakthrough, which is typical for oil-wet reservoirs. To further investigate this recovery trend, the in-situ contact angles were measured on the three different minerals in the rock. Fig. 6 shows the contact angle distribution on these minerals before and after brine flooding. The average contact angle of the rock after aging at Swi was about 139° on quartz, 144° on carbonate, and 140° on feldspar. Examples of in situ contact angles measured on different minerals before and after waterflooding are displayed in Fig. 7.

The average contact angle remained almost identical on all minerals after waterflooding, which suggested that the brine injection did not affect the wettability of the rock, and the rock remained oil-wet. Under these conditions, the oil removal is governed by a piston-like displacement mechanism [35]. Brine invaded the center of the pores and left the remaining oil either in the corner of the pores or as layers sandwiched between the aqueous phase in the corners and the aqueous phase in the center, as seen in Fig. 7. Most DNAPL was recovered during the first pore volume of brine injection, and brine was only able to displace oil in the large pores of Arkose. Due to the oil-wet condition, the threshold capillary pressure required to invade the pores needs to be high. Thus, oil removal would be difficult after the breakthrough has been achieved. In order to recover more oil, either an extensive amount of water had to be injected, or a much higher flow rate was required [36]. Neither of these two approaches is practical during real field practices.

Before nanofluid flooding, the initial water saturation was restored by injecting DNAPL into the core at a higher flow rate. The initial water saturation was re-established after three days of continuous oil injection to reach a value of 15.1%, which was comparable to 14.5% prior to waterflooding. Moreover, scans of the core after the first and second Swi provided very similar pore-scale fluid distribution maps. Here again, the core was scanned after the injection of 1 PV, 3 PV, 8 PV, and 20 PV of nanofluid. Fig. 5(b) shows the water saturation along the core, and Fig. 5(c) presents the cumulative recovery after different stages of the nanofluid flooding. The data indicate that 1 PV of nanofluid injection recovered a similar amount of DNAPL as 1 PV of brine (12.8% vs. 14.1%). However, the recovery increased 3-folds from 1 to 3 PV of nanofluid injection, with an additional 12% of DNAPL. Subsequently, the recovery rate was lowered with only 4.8% more oil from 3 to 8 PV. The final recovery was 37% after 20 PV of nanofluid flooding, which was 11% higher than that of waterflooding. The slope of the curve in Fig. 5(c) suggests that more DNAPL would have been recovered with a further injection of nanofluid.

Fig. 8(a) shows the in-situ contact angle distribution for all three minerals at various stages of nanofluid flooding. The contact angle variation between each stage is also provided in Fig. 8(b). Two striking observations could be made from these data: (i) the nanofluid was able to lower the contact angle by 5–20 degrees at each stage except between 1 and 3 PV of injection where the reduction exceeded 30°. This suggested that some soaking time was necessary for the wettability reversal to occur. (ii) Overall, wettability alteration was more pronounced on carbonate and feldspar than on quartz. It is likely that the GQD adsorbed more on carbonate and feldspar than on quartz, causing a greater change in contact angle. The behavior on carbonate was expected from the adsorption data in Fig. 4(b). Examples of in-situ contact angle measurements on all minerals at different stages are shown in Fig. 9.

After fluid injection, the residual DNAPL can be trapped inside the pores or extend over multiple internally connected pore elements in the form of small clusters. The sizes and connections of these clusters reflect important information about the displacement mechanisms inside the porous media. Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the cumulative contribution of trapped DNAPL cluster size volume versus cluster total volume, as well as the 3D visualization of these clusters at different cumulative portion levels, e.g., 20%, 40%, and over 40%. It should be noted that after base brine flooding, the largest cluster size is 8.33 × 107 μm3, which takes 52.8% of the total residual cluster volume. The distribution curve suggested a significantly non-uniform size distribution, which is a reflection of the oil-wet nature of the pores. After waterflooding, the brine only invaded in the center of the pores, leaving much oil residing in the corners, crevices, and oil layers on pore walls. However, after nanofluid flooding, the shape of the cluster size distribution curve is much different from that after waterflooding, with a shift to the left and a higher slope, indicating the presence of smaller clusters. This reduction in cluster size after nanofluid flooding is a strong indication of the wettability reversal effect of the nanofluid, which is also in agreement with the contact angle measurements conducted in the previous section. The cluster size reduction effect of the nanofluid is also illustrated in the 3D representative visualization models Fig. 10 (b). The clusters with the largest size, 40% and 20% cumulative total fractions after nanofluid flooding, are all smaller than their counterparts after waterflooding. Similar results were observed in another work. These observations agree well with previous studies [20].

Fig. 11 shows the 3D fluid occupancy map during water and nanofluid flooding. The 3D visualization of the pores is beneficial to gain valuable insights into the fluid-rock interactions at different stages of flooding. After 3 PV of injection, brine invaded only into the center of the DNAPL globule in the pore (Fig. 11 (a)), while the nanofluid displaced oil not only in the center but also in the corners of the pore (Fig. 11 (b)). Although the nanofluid displaced more DNAPL by invading crevices of the pore, it should be noted that this pore was still in oil-wet conditions. This observation is a strong sign that the IFT reduction effect of the nanofluid is an important mechanism in enhanced recovery that should not be ignored. At the end of the waterflooding, the brine was only able to displace oil from the center of the pore (Fig. 11 (c)). In contrast, the nanofluid invaded the entire pore and altered its wettability (Fig. 11 (d)).

Despite the nanofluid ability to lower the IFT, a similar recovery was obtained after the first PV of nanofluid and base brine injection, as seen in Fig. 5(c). This result was expected since the DNAPL phase used in this study had a high viscosity and density at ambient conditions. Upon fluid injection, fingering and breakthrough caused by the low mobility ratio would result in a poor sweep efficiency, which explains the very low recovery factors usually obtained in heavy oil reservoirs [37–39]. The nanofluid did not have sufficient contact time with the rock surface to alter its wettability during the early time of injection. This shows that IFT reduction is not enough to displace DNAPL. Wettability alteration is an important mechanism to improve the recovery of heavy oils. With subsequent nanofluid injection, the contact angle continuously decreased, suggesting that the wettability reversal effect was still in progress. After 20 PV injection, more NAPL was recovered by the nanofluid with further wettability alteration, while the NAPL recovery after waterflooding remained unchanged.

The recovery mechanisms can also be inferred by studying fluid displacement in pores of different sizes. Based on the pore size distribution map in Fig. 3(b), the pores were divided into three groups with equal volume (i.e., about a third of the total pore volume). Small, medium, and large pores had an equivalent diameter of 0–83 µm, 83–109 µm, and 109–220 µm, respectively. Some examples are illustrated in the inset of Fig. 12. These three types of pores had the same total volume but their numbers were different. To assess the efficacy of the nanofluid in displacing DNAPL, the difference in cumulative oil recovery between nanofluid and brine was calculated for each pore type and PV injection (Fig. 12). Interestingly, brine was more effective in invading large pores than the nanofluid after 1 PV of injection, which resulted in the slightly higher recovery seen in Fig. 5(c). However, the nanofluid was able to penetrate small pores that were not accessible to brine after 3 PV of injection due to both IFT and contact angle reduction, which lowered the threshold capillary pressure of these pores. This is consistent with the soaking time needed for GQD nanoparticles to alter the wettability of the mineral surfaces. Subsequently, more medium and large pores were invaded by the nanofluid as the injection continued, which led to an 11% increase in DNAPL recovery after 20 PV of nanofluid injection, as compared to waterflooding.

This study investigated the pore-scale displacement mechanism of a heavy crude oil in a heterogeneous aquifer rock using a novel nanofluid extracted from Wyoming sub-bituminous coal. The nanofluid consisted of a mixture of graphene quantum dots and engineered quantum dots with amphiphilic character. The mixture exhibited synergistic effects that allowed the nanoparticles to arrange at the oil/water interface and lower the IFT by 60% at concentrations as low as 0.1 wt% in brine. Miniature core flooding experiments integrated with x-ray microtomography were used to obtain pore-scale fluid occupancy maps after different pore volumes of fluid injection, from which saturation profiles were established, in-situ wettability of the most dominant minerals (quartz, carbonate, and feldspar) were measured, and residual cluster analysis was performed in pores of various sizes (small, medium, and large). Three-phase contact angle measurements revealed that: (i) the nanofluid reversed the wettability of all minerals from oil-wet to water-wet, especially on carbonates due to the tendency of quantum dots to adsorb on these surfaces, and (ii) the nanofluid required a certain soaking time to start altering the wettability of the rock. Analysis of the pore-scale fluid occupancy maps indicated that IFT reduction was not sufficient to displace the oil at the early stages of nanofluid flooding because of the heavy nature of this fluid. As a result, no noticeable impact of the nanofluid was observed after 1 PV. It was only after 3 PV of injection that the nanofluid could invade small pores that were not accessible to brine due to its ability to lower the IFT and contact angle. Brine stopped recovering DNAPL after 8 PV, whereas the nanofluid kept displacing more oil in small, medium, and large pores as the injection continued, leading to an 11% incremental recovery after 20 PV. This result is significant since the tests were performed with a heavy oil at ambient temperature. The performance of GQD-based nanofluids has the potential to be significantly enhanced in deep oil reservoirs where elevated temperatures would promote fluid mobilization due to reduced oil density and viscosity. The new insights provided by this study could be used to validate pore-scale network models and guide in the design and selection of more effective and environmental-friendly nanomaterials for the production of NAPL from heterogeneous rocks.

Kaustubh Rane: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data analysis and interpretation, Writing - original draft. Bingjun Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data analysis and interpretation, Writing - original draft. Lamia Goual: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the U.S. National Science Foundation, USA (NSF Career Award #1351296) for financial support and Dr. Mohammad Piri for providing access to the Center of Innovation for Flow through Porous Media (COIFPM). The authors also would like to thank Omar Elmasry for conducting the QEMSCAN analysis.
